blockdesc-blastfurnacedoor*: The iron door of the blast furnace.
blockdesc-hopperreinforced*: Combines crushed ores into blast mix for the bell hopper below.
blockdesc-hopperbell*: Drips stockpiled blast mix down into the furnace shaft.
blockdesc-blastfurnace-tuyere*: A hot blast intake for the furnace shaft.
blockdesc-blastfurnacetap*: Drains the furnace's molten reservoir into a canal below.
blockdesc-slag*: A block of solidified slag from the blast furnace.
blockdesc-solidifiediron*: Iron that cooled and hardened before it could be tapped or poured.
blockdesc-cowperstove-intake*: The intake face of a cowper stove.
blockdesc-smokestack-intake*: Vents surplus furnace exhaust to the sky.
blockdesc-moltencanal-start*: The anchor of a molten canal network.
blockdesc-moltencanal-bend*: A 90° bend in a molten canal run.
blockdesc-moltencanal-tap*: Pours metal out of the far end of a canal.
blockdesc-moltencanal-moldpedestal*: Fills a mold it from the canal network.
blockdesc-moltenbarrel*: Bulk storage for liquid metal or slag.
blockdesc-convertercontrol*: Used to operate the bessemer converter.
blockdesc-converterbessemer*: Massive vessel that blows molten iron into steel.
blockdesc-convertertransmission*: Uses axle's mechanical power to rotate the vessesl.
itemdesc-slag*: A by-product tapped from the blast furnace.
itemdesc-powderedslag*: Ground slag, future mortar or fertilizer.

bf-info-exhaustfull: Exhaust network is full! Production halted.
bf-info-needsmix: Blast Furnace needs more blast mix to fire!

game:ingameerror-smex-moldtoosmall: This mold is used with the mold pedestal.

advancedsteelmaking-text:
Steelmaking Expanded adds a full industrial pipeline for iron/steel production that works in two stages. In Stage I you will build a Blast Furnace and its support machinery to smelt iron in bulk. Stage II bolts a Bessemer Converter onto that line to refine the molten iron into steel. Build the iron stage first and get it running reliably — the steel stage is expensive and feeds directly off it.
Blast furnace requires tier3 refractory bricks, which means you need to reach the steel age before starting working on this! Be aware that all of this works only when powered with Steam so try building a steam power setup before attempting this.

The full production chain is described in four companion articles:
- The Blast Furnace — the heart of the operation and where you should start Stage I.
- Hot Blast & Gas Handling — describes cowper stoves, the smoke stack and interaction with pipe network. You cannot fire furnace and achieve iron melting point without these!
- Molten Metal & Casting — work with canals, taps and molds to produce desired items. Route overflow molten metal or waste slag into special barrels.
- The Bessemer Converter — Stage II begins here, allowing you to use heavy machinery to blow iron into steel. Utilizes all previous mechanics so be ready!

The pipes, boilers and steam engines that power it all come from the companion mod — see Steam Power. These are utilized to produce mechanical power and to pressurize air to required pressure so it could work as blast.

Here is the quick brief of the complete production run for iron:
1. Stock the Reinforced Hopper of the Blast Furnace with crushed iron ore, crushed coke and lime, and enable dropping of the Bell Hopper.
2. Let the shaft of the furnace fill with blast mix piles, after which ignite it with a torch, and close the door.
3. Push Air through a Heated up Cowper Stove into the Tuyeres as hot blast (a powered Air Blower on a Steam Engine provides the required air and pressure). Vent excess gases through the Smoke Stack.
4. Watch the temperature climb past ~1482 °C into the Melting phase while alternating your Two Cowper Stoves so the blast never goes cold.
5. When iron and slag have pooled, open the Molten Metal Taps to drain them down the canals into Molds or Barrels. Be ware it can solidify!
6. Keep the hopper supplied, the exhaust flowing, boilers boiling and repeat.
handbook-blastfurnace-text:
The Blast Furnace
The blast furnace is the heart of the operation: a tall multiblock built mostly from refractory bricks, with an iron door, two tuyeres (air intakes) low on the shaft and two gas outlets high up. Place the door block and shift + rightmouse it to see the build outline, then fill in every highlighted block until the structure reports complete.

Blocks required:
- 98 x Refractory Bricks (Tier 3)
- 1 x Blast Furnace Door (the control block, placed first)
- 2 x Tuyere
- 2 x Molten Metal Tap
- 2 x Gas Outlet (Refractory brick Tier3)
- 1 x Reinforced Hopper
- 1 x Bell Hopper
The hollow shaft inside is left empty — the hoppers fill it with blast mix.

Feeding it. The furnace does not eat ore directly! It burns Blast Mix, a packed pile of ore and fuel. You cannot craft blast mix by hand — it is produced by the pair of hoppers stacked above the shaft. The Reinforced Hopper is the magazine: open it (rightmouse) and load three things — crushed iron ore, crushed coke, and lime (flux). Crush iron ore and coke in a pulverizer, coke is baked from coal in a coke oven, and even scrap iron bits can be crushed back into crushed iron for resmelting. The hopper combines 12 crushed iron + 3 crushed coke + 1 lime into 16 blast mix and stockpiles it internally (total 60 units of iron).

Below it sits the Bell Hopper, which drips the stockpiled blast mix down into the shaft. Toggle dropping with ctrl + rightmouse on either or the hoppers. The dropping stops on its own once the furnace is full.

Fuel pile broken? Blast mix behaves just like a coal pile: rightmouse the ground to lay loose mix back down, stack more onto it, and feed it back to the furnace through hoppers. It even burns as a normal fuel in a pinch, but don't expect much from it.

Firing it. Let the bell hopper fill the furnace, after which ignite the blast mix inside, and close the door. Once every pile in the hearth is burning, the door is shut and the exhaust outlets are not choked, the furnace begins firing and climbs toward ~1400 °C on its own, but you need to hold it above iron's melting point (~1482 °C, see the Hot Blast article) for a few minutes and it enters the Melting phase, converting blast mix into a reservoir of molten iron and molten slag.

The fire is fragile! Opening the door, running out of blast mix, a choked or backfed exhaust, or letting the molten reservoir overflow will all snuff it out — and an extinguished melt solidifies in the hearth as iron blocks you must mine out. Keep the exhaust flowing and the door shut while it works the iron.
handbook-hotblast-text:
What is hot blast? Preheated air under pressure that allows chemical processes and smelting occur inside the furnace. For the purposes of this production, blast is air under pressure higher than 2.5 atm and temperature higher than 800 °C.

Why hot blast? A raw blast furnace tops out around 1400 °C — not enough to melt iron. To push past that you reclaim the heat it throws away through exhaust.

The gas network. Everything breathes through pipes (from Pipes and Power Expanded). In this situation we will work with gas in three kinds: ordinary Air, hot Exhaust spilling from the furnace, and forced Blast (pressurized air). You need to setup a few specific machines and structures for this to work. For the air/exhaust you will need:
- The Air Blower — a steam-engine sub-machine that pressurizes Air into Blast. This is what forces gas through the cowper stoves and into the furnace through tuyeres.
- Valves open and close a line by hand — essential for swapping the cowper stoves around and controlling the flow inside pipe networks.
- Pressure valves open automatically above their gate pressure, allowing overflow to another side (input side is marked with copper textured rim).
- Outlets connect the network to a structure's face and are a part of their multiblock footprint.
Each network tracks its gas type, volume and temperature; an open-ended pipe leaks. Think carefully how many machines you need so you won't under or over supply!

The Cowper Stove — a heat regenerator. Pipe the furnace's Exhaust into a Cowper Stove and it soaks that heat into its heatsink at the core ("charging"); burning a coal pile inside charges it faster, with anthracite fastest. Beware: burning low grades of coal makes the stove itself produce exhaust from its main output!
Once charged, route plain Blast through the same stove and it pours out scorching hot. Feed that into the furnace tuyeres and the internal temperature will start rising again toward ~1700 °C.

Blocks required (per stove — you want two):
- 48 x Refractory Bricks (any tier)
- 1 x Cowper Stove Intake (the control block)
- 4 x Heat Sink
- 2 x Gas Outlet (any refractory)
- 1 x Gas Passthrough (any refractory)
- 1 x Iron Hatch Door
One slot below the intake is left open for the coal pile that speeds up the heat exchange.

A single stove only does one job at a time, and a charged core runs down as you draw blast from it. Run two stoves as a pair: while one charges on exhaust, the other discharges hot blast for the furnace. When the discharging one cools down below 800 °C, swap their roles with valves. Keep exhaust and air on separate paths — if they mix inside a stove it cannot heat up.

The Smoke Stack. A blast furnace produces more exhaust than the stoves can swallow. The Smoke Stack vents the surplus to the sky — a backed-up exhaust line chokes and extinguishes the furnace, so treat it as the safety valve of the whole system.
Blocks required:
- 1 x Smoke Stack Intake (the control block)
- 24 x Refractory Bricks (any tier)
- 36 x Any Bricks

Don't forget about the Stoves! All of this doesn't operate on it's own and you need to keep an eye on the stoves' temperature. Otherwise your furnace may stop smelting iron or even extinguish!
handbook-casting-text:
Molten Canals. Liquid metal is plumbed, not carried. Lay a Molten Canal (Start) as the anchor of a network, then run straights, bends and junctions from it — they are crafted from cobblestone and fire clay. Drain the furnace with its Molten Metal Taps — the lower tap pours iron, the upper tap pours slag. There should be a start of a canal below it, otherwise it won't allow you to toggle it with rightmouse. A Canal Tap at the far end pours the metal into barrel, if you have excess material and need to drain the canals. (Note that molten iron flow is simulated so not everything may be drained.)

Sealing — a manual valve. rightmouse an empty straight canal with 4 fire clay and it caps over, severing the network at that block. Only a drained canal can be sealed. Reopen it with a chisel (recovers 2 fire clay). This can be used to manually select which canal path the metal will take, if you have multiple possible ways for it to go.

Mind the temperature. Metal in the canals slowly cools! If it drops too far it solidifies in place. A hardened canal stops flowing and must be chipped clear. Use Chisel + Hammer once it cools enough to recover most of the metal as bits, and those bits can be crushed back into crushed iron for the furnace. Keep runs short, keep them fed, and empty them before they or the furnace go cold.

Casting. A canal tap pours into two destinations:
- A Mold Pedestal casts small tool molds: shift + rightmouse places or removes the mold, ctrl + rightmouse toggles pouring. This mod adds larger casting molds — plate mold, quad rod mold and double ingot mold that you clay-form and fire like any tool mold. The big anvil and helve hammer molds go under the canal tap itself instead.
- A Molten Barrel — bulk storage for liquid metal. Use it to buffer excess iron, or to hold slag from the top tap. When contents solidify, you can also chisel it out like solidified canal, simply place the barrel on the ground first.

Handling hot molds. A freshly filled mold is dangerously hot! Carrying it bare-handed burns you (wear heavy leather or blacksmith's gloves). While the metal is still liquid, the mold can only ride in your empty active hand — stashing it in a bag, or racking it spills the metal instantly. Set it down and let it harden, then collect the casting with a plain rightmouse.

Slag is not waste. Solidified slag grinds into powdered slag: mixed with slaked lime in a barrel it yields mortar, and spread on farmland it acts as a phosphate fertilizer — furnace byproduct into building stock and crop yield.
handbook-bessemer-text:
What is the Bessemer Process? It is a way you can turn raw iron into steel. The idea is that iron contains to much pollution in form of excess carbon, sulfur and other elements. You need to literally boil the iron by blowing blast through it, firing out these pollutants and purifying iron to the state of mild steel.

The Bessemer Converter is the machine that takes molten iron and blows it into steel. It is the most demanding machine in the mod — it draws both mechanical power and a blast supply, and only works as the final stage of an already-running molten line. Do not attempt it until your iron tier is established!

Building it. The converter is a 3x3x3 vessel raised through right-click construction. Place its control block first, then rightmouse it with 4 rusty gears and 12 iron or steel rods in your hotbar to start the vessel, and build up the construction stages until it reports complete. Around the vessel sit its service ports: a gas intake, a mechanical transmission, an input tap, and an output canal start block.

Blocks required:
- 1 x Bessemer Control (the control block, placed first)
- 1 x Bessemer Converter — raised in place from 4 rusty gears + 12 iron/steel rods
- 1 x Bessemer Transmission
- 1 x Bessemer Gas Intake
- 1 x Molten Canal (Start)
- 2 x Molten Canal (Straight)
- 1 x Molten Canal (Tap)

Construction cost for the converter vessel itself. All stages together consume:
- 40 x Iron/Steel Plate
- 42 x Iron/Steel Nails & Strips
- 18 x Iron/Steel Rod
- 84 x Refractory Brick (tier 3)
- 84 x Fire Clay
- 3 x Gas Piping (Straight)

Powering it. Two services from the systems you already built:
- Mechanical power. Connect an axle to the transmission so the vessel can tilt and blow. Recommended to use steam engine to power this due to torque required.
- Blast. Pipe pressured air from a blower into the gas intake of the converter. The intake must face the same way as the control block or it won't couple.
Then run a molten canal from a furnace tap into the converter's input tap, and a second canal below will carry its output toward your molds.

Operating it.
1. Set the converter to Filling and pour molten iron in from the canal until charged to your desired amount (max amount not required for operation).
2. Switch to Normal. With power and blast flowing, it refines the charge at ~1800 °C for about five minutes into steel.
3. Set it to Pouring to drain the finished steel into the output canal — from there it travels the molten network exactly like iron.

The same temperature rule applies inside the vessel! If the charge cools and solidifies before you pour it, break the converter with a steel pickaxe to recover the metal, then rebuild. So make sure you can drain the vessel before it solidifies.

Notes. It is required to have minimum two converters to consume all of the output iron of a single blast furnace. You may start with one but if you truly want to achieve continuous steel production operate two converters. All of the machinery required for production take a lot of space, both horizontal and vertical. Also, make sure that you can access easily all of the valves and controls, so you won't waste time trying to reach something while your molten metal solidifies or furnace cools. This may be the time you finally built that factory you always wanted to have!
